content.full_text:
Fern Poltrrcal PnacrICES CouuIssloN
428 J Street . Suite 620 . Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 r Fax (9t6) 322-0886
September 17,2009
Daniel Miller, Filing Officer
County of Marin
P.O. Box E
San Rafael, CA 94913
Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-09-189
Dear Mr. Miller:
This letter is in response to your request on behalf of Marin County for advice regarding
the campaign filing officei duty provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").r
QUESTIONS
1. Must a filing officer question a committee that reports a substantial sum in
unitemized contributions during a review of campaign statements?
2. Can the filing officer, or a member of the public, have access to a committee's
contribution records to ensure compliance with the Act?
3. Must a filing officer pruvide public access to its written communications with
candidates and committees? May your office post such communiqu6s on your website and"/or
your agency's intemal public access program?
CONCLUSIONS
l. No, the Act does not require filing officers to question a committee that reports
receiving a substantial sum in unitemized contributions.
2. The Act does not require a committee to provide its contribution records to a filing
I The political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014' All statutory
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices
Commission are contained in Sections l8l l0 through I 8997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.
6,*.=i


File No. A-09-189
Page No. 2
3. Written communications between a filing officer and a candidate or committee are
not public records under Section 81008. However, such correspondence may be subject to the
Public Records Act.
FACTS
You are the filing officer in Marin County whose duty is to review campaign statements.
You have posed questions concerning your filing officer responsibilities with respect to
campaign statements filed by committees which are reporting a large amount of unitemized
contributions. You provided a sample of a recent filing reporting $20,328 in unitemized
contributions by the Marin Professional Firefighters PAC.
ANALYSIS
Duty to File
Candidates and committees are required to file campaign statements disclosing
contributions received and expenditures made (Section 84200 et seq.). The Act requires that
campaign statements for local committees be filed with the local filing officer (Section 84215).
Contents of Campaign Statements
The Act requires committees to report the total amount of contributions received.
However, the law provides that sources that have given a cumulative amount of less than $100 in
a calendar year are not identified. Itemization of the name, address, and, if applicable, the
occupation and employer of contributors is required when a person contributes $100 or more in a
calendar year (Secti on 84211). Therefore, if a committee receives contributions from a number
of sources and each source contributes less than $100 in the calendar year, the campaign report
will not identify such persons. [n fact, the campaign reports provide a specific line item that only
requires the total of all unitemized contributions.
It may be of interest to note that it is not uncommon to see large amounts of unitemized
contributions reported on campaign reports. This often occurs when organizations receive a
large number contributions through membership dues or payroll deductions but the amount
donated by each source does not exceed $100 in the calendar year.
Duties of the Filtng Officer
As filing officer for Marin County elections, campaign statements for candidates and
committees active in your jurisdiction are filed with your office. The Act requires that you
review those campaign statements to determine whether they confonn on their face with the
requirements of the Act. The Act does not require that you verify the accuracy of the entries
provided by the filers. [n fact, Regulation 181l0(bXlXA) specifically states that the filing
officer is not required to "Seek or obtain information to verify entries on a campaign statement."


File No. A-09-189
Page No. 3
Therefore, as a general rule, you are not required to question a committee's disclosure of large
amounts of unitemized contributions. Please note there may be extenuating circumstances in
which it may be clear on its face that a candidate or committee is avoiding disclosure of
contributors. If that should occur, you should call the FPPC for advice.
Public Records
Every report and statement filed pursuant to the Act is a public record (Section 81008).
While the Act requires candidates and committees to maintain detailed accounts, records, bills
and receipts, nothing in the Act requires that the committee provide such records to assist a filing
officer to perform reviewing duties or to a member of the public. (Section 84104.) If a
committee is subject to audit or enforcement investigation, it must make these records available
to auditors. Additionally, while the Act requires that the filing officer supply the necessary
forms and manuals to filers as well as notify filers of their filing obligations it does not require
that those correspondences be treated as public records. However, other provisions of law
outside of our jurisdiction, most notably the Public Records Act, may govern these issues.
(Section 6250, et seq.) Therefore, you may want to consider reviewing those provisions.
Additionally, the Act does not restrict or prohibit a filing officer from posting written
communications he or she sends in the course of performing filing officer duties. However, we
cannot advise whether other laws or policies outside the Act may place restrictions.
If you have any other questions regarding this matter, please contact me
at (916) 322-5660.
Sincerely
Scott Hallabrin
General Counsel
By:
Political Reform Consultant
Technical Assistance Division
SAJjgl
